data.title: FVAL: GARP Tilt Secures Its Edge Over IVV (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
In my view, the Fidelity Value Factor ETF (FVAL) offers a combination of decent value and healthy growth characteristics that together translate into a GARP tilt (i.e., a 0.93 weighted average PEG ratio), which will secure its outperformance versus the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV) this year. So I upgraded FVAL to a Buy from the Hold rating I assigned to it in 2022.

The gist is that even though the oil market does not quite believe that the Strait of Hormuz situation will be resolved soon enough, as, sadly, there is no visible progress in the peace talks, I believe that investors will continue ignoring the short-term disruptions for two reasons.

  • First, the focus is currently on the upcoming tech earnings, which I hope will be impressive enough to boost sentiment further.
  • Second, there is still hope that the peace talks will bear fruit and the waterway will reopen fully, which will trigger a quick decline in oil benchmarks, with the physical market following.

Besides, I believe that one of the narratives that has been first protecting the U.S. market from a severe drawdown and then contributing to its quick recovery is that the U.S. economy is more or less immunized (though a statement that it is completely immune is a very dangerous fallacy) from the supply shock given its immense crude oil production, which, according to the U.S. Energy Information Administration (EIA), stood at 13.246 million bpd as of January, and reserves.

In this regard, it makes sense to assume that given this decisive advantage, global investors will be flocking to U.S. equities, ignoring the fear that a more expensive WTI crude will push prices for distillates into the territory where their impact on inflation will be very much visible, requiring the Fed to step in and increase interest rates (and a bear market will be an inevitable consequence). I view this as a significant tailwind that can keep multiples at high levels, which, in combination with robust earnings growth, means more gains for the S&P 500.

So, considering this backdrop, I expect growthier portfolios to beat those overweight in value and low volatility. However, given uncertainty is still high, it is important to be selective, which is why I favor GARP over pure growth.

FVAL Strategy

As we know from the Fidelity website, FVAL

...seeks to provide investment returns that correspond, before fees and expenses, generally to the performance of the Fidelity U.S. Value Factor IndexSM.

It is explained that the index

... is designed to reflect the performance of stocks of large and mid-capitalization U.S. companies that have attractive valuations.

According to the summary prospectus available on the Fidelity website, the index

...is constructed using Fidelity's rules-based proprietary index methodology. Stocks are ranked within each sector and given a composite score based on four measures of value: high free-cash-flow yield; low enterprise value to earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization; low price to tangible book value; and low price to future earnings. Within each sector, composite scores are adjusted based on market capitalization. Stocks with the highest composite scores within each sector are identified for inclusion in the index.

Importantly, we also know that the index adheres to the principle of sector neutrality:

Each sector is neutral-weighted relative to the broader equity market. Within each sector, each stock is weighted based on its market capitalization in the broader equity market plus an overweight adjustment that is equal for all constituents within that sector.

FVAL Portfolio: GARP Tilt To Secure Outperformance

As of April 23, there were 123 common stocks in the FVAL portfolio, with the top ten accounting for approximately 39.62% of the net assets and NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) occupying the top spot with a 7.69% weight. Approximately 46.27% of its net assets are allocated to S&P 500 companies, as per my calculations. And its sector exposures are mostly similar to those of IVV, with one notable nuance: FVAL is underweight in information technology by 4.25%.

In terms of the factor mix, which is a lynchpin of my thesis, what I find most advantageous is that, thanks to the strategy that does not push too hard when it comes to value screening and maintains sector neutrality, the FVAL portfolio is currently sporting a tilt towards the growth-at-a-reasonable-price style.

More specifically, although its weighted average market cap is even larger than IVV's (mainly because it is overweight in Broadcom Inc. (AVGO)), its adjusted WA earnings yield is stronger by around 1.33%, while P/S and EV/EBITDA are slightly more comfortable. And its allocation to stocks with a B- Quant Valuation grade or higher is also solid, especially considering we are talking about a portfolio heavy in mega caps.

Obviously, FVAL is anything but a growth ETF, and its portfolio does not offer weighted average growth rates that would dwarf those of IVV, especially when it comes to the forward EBITDA growth rate. However, thanks to its substantial exposure to value and also to its lower allocation to IT, I would argue that FVAL has a GARP tilt, which is confirmed by the PEG, Price/Sales-to-Growth, and EV/EBITDA-to-Growth ratios.

For clarity, the PEG ratio below 1, which I calculated by dividing the weighted average TTM P/E ratio by the forward EPS growth rate, is the territory where GARP investors believe decent opportunities reside.

Next, what I also like is that FVAL has a considerable allocation to high-quality stocks, as over 90% of its net assets are deployed to companies with a B- Quant Profitability grade or higher.

But there is something to dislike, too. For example, speaking of low volatility, the factor that I expect to underperform this year, as it restrains upside capture during bull markets, FVAL has a little larger exposure to it than IVV, which is illustrated by its WA 24-month and 60-month beta coefficients. To give a bit more context on that, 42.34% of its net assets are allocated to stocks with a 24-month beta below 1. In IVV, such names have a 38.9% weight. So there is a risk that this factor will detract from its total return, hindering it from delivering the second consecutive year of outperformance.

Besides, I am a little disappointed by FVAL's almost 10% smaller exposure to stocks with at least a B- Quant EPS Revisions grade.

FVAL Performance

Since its inception in September 2016, FVAL has quite confidently outperformed such peers as the iShares S&P 500 Value ETF (IVE), the Invesco S&P 500 Pure Value ETF (RPV), and the iShares Russell 1000 Value ETF (IWD).

On the negative side, its returns have not been robust enough to rival those of IVV and the iShares Russell 1000 ETF (IWB).

Upon closer inspection, we see a few positives. For instance, FVAL outperformed IVV in 4 out of 9 full calendar years it has in the books, including 2017, 2021, 2022, and 2025. It also outmaneuvered the S&P 500 ETF in January and March of 2026, as it was better positioned to tackle headwinds from the Iran war and soaring oil prices.

Period Active Return (IVV used as a benchmark)
October–December 2016 3.7%
2017 0.7%
2018 -3.4%
2019 -0.9%
2020 -9.3%
2021 1.6%
2022 3.8%
2023 -3.2%
2024 -6.9%
2025 1.7%
January–March 2026 0.8%

Created by the author using data from Portfolio Visualizer

Next, from October 2016 to March 2026, FVAL delivered the highest active return (with IVV selected as a benchmark) compared to such value ETFs as IVE, RPV, and IWD.

Besides, its risk-adjusted returns (the Sharpe and Sortino ratios) were also the strongest in the value ETF cohort.

In sum, while FVAL's past performance can be used as an argument against it (the truth is, it has trailed IVV since inception), I am still gravitating towards the view that 2026 may become a new 2021 (when the capital rotation drove its outperformance) or 2025 for it.

Final Thoughts

While I define myself as a value-focused investor, I believe in flexibility and adaptability. In other words, it makes no sense to stick with an underperforming factor forever, and, for instance, if the addition of a growth ingredient is merited, there is no reason to avoid it. In this regard, I suppose FVAL offers an interesting approach at the intersection of value and growth, producing a clear GARP tilt, which I hope will secure its outperformance vs. IVV this year.

Of course, there are risks. The key one here is that we might be underestimating the magnitude of oil supply disruption or pricing in too rosy expectations about de-escalation. Depending on how long the traffic in the Strait of Hormuz is not restored to the pre-war levels, the impact on prices of distillates, inflation, and even economic growth can be severe, driving not just a correction but probably a bear market. And in a worst-case scenario, given FVAL has a high correlation to the S&P 500, I believe its maximum drawdown can exceed 20%.

data.title: Apple: A New Boss And A New And Higher Dividend (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
These are certainly uncertain times, which would make it tempting to exit the stock market entirely and hide in government bonds. I would argue, however, that a wiser move is to move some of your capital into Apple Inc. (AAPL) instead. You get a steadily growing dividend, you get a relatively high level of safety, and you are still exposed to long-term growth of your capital. Hence, I rate Apple a Buy this year, as opposed to a Sell in my article last year.

I was right for a time on my Sell rating last year as AI fears and geopolitical uncertainty persisted throughout the spring and early summer. But from late summer, my pessimism was proved wrong, as the stock moved right up. It is just hard to keep a good company down, I guess.

Yes, there are some down periods in this chart, but the general trend is steadily upwards. Five years ago, the stock traded at $131, whereas it now hovers around $271—for a gain of 107% or an average annual return of 15.6%. When we add in the dividend, we arrive at just over 16%. Pretty solid performance for a company that is one of the largest in the world. There is constant skepticism around this company, but it just keeps on delivering.

It will be a feat indeed if the company can continue delivering such returns over the next five years. I do not think it will deliver at such high rates, but I do believe it will be able to crank out total returns in excess of 10%—which is reason enough to have the stock in your portfolio.

Apple's Dividend History

Apple has a varied dividend history. It did pay a quarterly dividend way back in the day, but it abruptly stopped in late 1995. As the company eventually turned around, profits and the stock both increased—but not the dividend. After some years the stock count increased, via a share split in 2000, but not the dividend. In 2005 the number of stocks increased again—but not the dividend. In October 2011, the world received some bad news: Steve Jobs was gone, and Apple investors worried about the future of Apple without Steve. He had always been clear on one thing: no dividends on his watch! Less than a year later, however, investors woke up to the news that not only had profits and the stock count increased, but now also a dividend! In July 2012, a $2.65 dividend was finally declared.

Adjusted for splits, the dividend was $0.095 back then and is currently at $0.26 and consequently is up by 2.75x over those years. It seems impressive at first glance, but it translates into an average annual growth rate of 8%. Certainly nothing to complain about, but perhaps lower than many dividend growth investors would have expected if they knew how fast this company would grow earnings over that same period.

Over the last five years, the dividend growth rate has regrettably decreased to an average annual increase of 5%. It is dependable; it beats inflation, but it does not stir up excitement. If we look at the orange line in the chart above, we can see that the payout ratio is not conservative—it is ultra-conservative. Most companies have erratic payout ratios because of one-offs, but over at Apple it consistently stays between 13% and 16% over the whole five-year span. I don't know how better to express that this dividend is safe!

Most dividend growth investors who have followed the name for a while know that the emphasis has been on stock buybacks and not dividend growth. Strictly tax-wise, it is a smart strategy, and in theory, investors should be agnostic whether they receive a dividend or sell some stocks to receive some income, but in reality, most people do not wish to deplete their holdings. Another critique in general against buybacks is that companies tend to do them when they have cash on hand and not necessarily when the stock is particularly cheap. In Apple's case, however, it has worked since the company, and hence the stock, has performed well over time, making historical buybacks profitable.

In the last fiscal year, it bought back $89.3 billion worth of stock, which is 5.8x more than the $15.4 billion in dividends. The company is not stingy, but I suspect a lot of its investors would prefer slightly lower buybacks and higher dividends instead. At the end of the day, the good news is that the dividend is safe; investors can rest assured that it will continue growing well into Dividend King status, and the total shareholder return is fairly generous.

Apple's Fundamentals

One could argue fundamentals are not too important in this case, as a company with a 13% payout ratio doesn't need any earnings growth in order to grow its dividend. On the other hand, the low payout ratio is a clear indication that, Steve Jobs or not, this Board is extremely conservative when it comes to distributing dividends. As a result, if earnings were flat or declining, a conservative Board would likely also hold back on dividend growth.

So on that note, let's look at the fundamentals of this juggernaut. In Apple's Q1 announcement, it reported an all-time record for both revenue and earnings per share with all-time high revenues for both the iPhone and Services. That Services is doing well is not a surprise, as this tends to grow over time as people store more and more data with Apple. But the stellar performance of the iPhone is a little surprising, given all the negativity in recent years and especially all the uncertainty about its prospects in China.

All the trade uncertainty surrounding the U.S. and China has been seen as a major risk for Apple. It seems, though, that it manages to navigate this uncertain environment in a commendable manner. As a matter of fact, the iPhone is taking market share in China and is almost as large as Huawei. The fears about Apple not being innovative enough for the Chinese market or Chinese forgoing American products were overblown, it seems. If it manages to do so well in such an environment, it bodes really well for the long-term prospects in the country for Apple.

When digging into the raw Apple Q1 numbers, we see that sales in Greater China are up a whopping 38% from last year to $25.5 billion, compared to an increase of 16% for sales in total. All regions are growing, which means that the popularity of the company's products is not confined to some areas of the world but is truly global.

If we look at sales by category, we see that the iPhone is doing incredibly well with an increase of 23% to $85 billion. Mac, iPad, and Wearables are treading water, whereas Services see some real growth of 14%. The good news for Services is that with the massive increase in iPhone sales, Services are likely to enjoy a tailwind as a larger installed base will drive more demand for Services connected to those devices. It seems, once again, that the fear of Apple not being innovative enough and the iPhone being passé was wrong.

The fear over the last two years or so has been that Apple is once again a bit late to catch the new fad, this time artificial intelligence. It did announce Apple Intelligence back in October 2024, but it didn't really do the trick. It failed to wow the public and gave the impression Apple has often given before: it is seldom at the vanguard but takes a deep breath and sees where things are going before making investments into new products and features. It makes us feel it is not on top of things, but usually it turns out favorably for the company. The advantage is that it doesn't spend a lot of money on capex into uncertain projects but can invest when it is more confident of the usefulness of the new technology.

The downside is that you don't grab the headlines, and it risks being seen as a slow innovator. On balance, though, it has tended to work out quite well for Apple. And I think we are seeing that again now. Instead of panicking and pouring tens of billions into AI in order to be first, they have let others do that and rely on its ubiquitousness to be a relevant partner for the leading AI labs if that turns out to be the best way forward, or rely on its cash pile to do later investments if that is the more profitable route to take.

I do expect some reservations as to the recently announced leadership change. John Ternus is unquestionably a hardware guy, which is very central to what Apple has always been. Even so, the question is if that is the right focus going forward, with all the rapid developments on the software and AI side of things. The counterargument would be that Apple certainly does not lack talent within software, and with AI models becoming more commoditized, what can really add value is the hardware interface to the consumer. If Apple can continue to be the leader—and maybe increase its lead—that is where the big differentiator lies.

This transition is very orderly; all the leading employees will continue at Apple, just in different roles, so I am not worried. I think the gradual introduction of AI into all of its products and a relentless focus on world-leading hardware will lay the ground for many more years of double-digit EPS growth rates.

Upcoming Dividend Hike

With all the solid fundamentals in mind, let us have a look into the potential coming dividend increase. In late April or early May each year, Apple announces its dividend in conjunction with its earnings release. This year the earnings release is on Thursday, April 30. If the future is like the past, the company will also announce a dividend increase that day.

As we saw under Apple's Dividend History section, the dividend growth rate has not been particularly high in recent years. This is despite the company doing really well. As a consequence, the payout ratio is extremely low, leaving lots of room for dividend growth if the Board so chooses. In fiscal year 2025, the EPS was up by 22.6%, and in Q1 it was up by 18.3%. The potential for a massive dividend increase is there. In theory, there is nothing wrong with bumping it in line with the EPS growth, that is, by 18-20%. The payout ratio would then remain low, and there would be plenty of money left for buybacks and investments.

Alas, with this conservative Board I do not think that will happen. It would deviate too much from established historical norms. At the other end of the scale, the absolute lowest increase it could go for is a one-cent increase to $0.27—or 3.8%. In dollar terms, that would be equal to the increase in recent years. To be honest, such a puny increase would be disappointing. With things going so well, with those enormous buybacks, and with such a low payout ratio, it would be completely unnecessary to offer its investors so little. I therefore think this is the year when the Board will go for a $0.02 increase for a new quarterly dividend of $0.28—a 7.7% increase. Such an increase would be below the EPS growth rate and would thus still be conservative enough for the Board, but would also reward shareholders at a level that I think is more appropriate.

Risk Factors

One obvious risk factor for Apple is competition, particularly in the Chinese market. Although iPhones are selling very well in China, Huawei is still larger, and the pace of innovation is furious. Apple needs to constantly innovate and stay on top of its game in order to keep its position.

Another risk is geopolitical fragmentation, which could lead to Apple having to shift more production to India, which might not be as economically advantageous as keeping it in China. That is the supply side, but the demand side is also fraught with risk. If trade tensions between the U.S. and China escalate, consumers in China might not want to buy iPhones; they might get tariffs on them, or they might be banned altogether. Although CEO Tim Cook has proved adept at navigating politics, the risk is not going away.

Lastly, we have currency risk. Lately, the U.S. dollar (DXY) has been weakening, improving U.S. reported earnings when foreign currency is converted to U.S. dollars. But for many years the exchange rate moved the other way, reducing earnings when reported in U.S. dollars. As a global company, Apple cannot avoid this risk but is also large enough that it can easily handle these fluctuations. It also has certain pricing power and can adjust the prices on its products if local currencies go the wrong way.

Current Valuation

Even great companies do not always turn out to be great investments if the price paid was too high. To that end, let's have a look at the valuation level of Apple compared to two competitors. As peers, I have chosen Samsung (SSNLF) for its smartphone business and Microsoft (MSFT) for its software and services business.

Apple Samsung Microsoft
Price/Sales 8.5x 2.3x 9.6x
Price/Earnings 31.8x 32.5x 24.8x
Yield 0.4% 0.8% 0.8%

Source: Seeking Alpha.

It would be a stretch to say that any of these are cheap, but seldom do you get world-class companies at a discount. Considering the dominant positions of these companies and the relatively solid growth rates, the price can be justified. The Price/Sales category is won by Samsung, with a multiple considerably below its American competitors. On Price/Earnings, Apple and Samsung are priced fairly similarly, while Microsoft is noticeably cheaper. Microsoft's multiple has contracted over the last year, driven primarily by fear of the enormous AI-related capital expenditures and whether those will pay off.

When it comes to the dividend yield, Microsoft is once again doing pretty well, together with Samsung. Sure, the yield is low, but relatively speaking, it is twice as high as Apple's. As we have seen in my analysis, Apple prioritizes buybacks instead of dividends, so it is to be expected that it is somewhat lower. In total, Apple came in second place in two categories and third place in the last one. I would say that while these companies are not cheap, one has to bear in mind the scale of the businesses and their growth prospects.

Wall Street analysts expect long-term EPS growth at Apple to be 11.8%. If we assume no change to the earnings multiple and add in the 0.4% dividend yield, we arrive at expected total annual returns of 12.2%. Though lower than the 16% over the last five years, getting double-digit expected returns from a global juggernaut should be attractive to conservative dividend growth investors.

Conclusion

Apple is a solid dividend payer with an ultra-conservative payout ratio. In an uncertain world, this name offers relative safety. Your capital appears secure in a company with a rock-solid balance sheet and strong—and getting ever stronger—underlying earnings. Though the dividend yield is low, shareholder yield is attractive, driven by enormous buybacks. With double-digit long-term earnings growth expected, this seems a safe bet for long-term dividend growth investors.

data.title: Texas Instruments: The Backbone Of The Data Center Revolution
data.description:
Texas Instruments Incorporated (TXN) is gaining momentum in the market as data center developments and supporting power infrastructure remain in high-growth mode. With the global data center capacity expected to double by 2030, I believe TXN’s market opportunity is significantly undervalued, particularly as the firm expands domestic production to facilitate growth in this market. With a high growth potential, I am reiterating my Strong Buy rating for TXN shares with a price target of $415/share at 23.64x eFY28 EV/aEBITDA.

Texas Instruments Incorporated Operational Update

TXN reported a robust Q1 ’26 driven by increased investments for semiconductors across the communications, data center, and electrification markets, driving substantial growth in both analog and embedded processing.

The data center market may be a major growth driver for TXN over the next decade, particularly when considering the complexity of power distribution across the data center site. A major factor to consider is that data centers are being developed at a larger scale, some at 200 MW to over 1 GW of capacity, requiring substantial power orchestration from the substation to the server racks.

Another factor is the coming transition to 800-VDC in 2027, which may deliver a major boost to TXN’s industrials market share. With many sites utilizing on-site power generation, power storage systems, substations, and switchgear, as well as power distribution on the server rack, TXN’s diversified portfolio should accelerate growth. I believe the turn towards onsite power generation may lead to a stronger growth trajectory for TXN, particularly when considering the orchestration and intricacies between power production and onsite battery storage systems, potentially requiring more infrastructure to support load capacity. JLL forecasts that $1-2T will be spent in outfitting the data centers with compute and networking infrastructure, delivering a major tailwind to connected server racks that will require supporting power infrastructure.

Management remains prudent in sustaining a flexible inventory to meet customer demand. As of Q1 ’26, TXN had 218 days of inventory on hand, a major drawdown from 231 days in the previous quarter. This should provide TXN with a certain degree of pull with the data center market, particularly as capacity is expected to grow substantially over the coming years. A major value driver TXN may have over peers like Monolithic Power Systems, Inc. (MPWR) is its U.S. operations, which are expected to expand in the coming years, facilitated by upwards of $1.6 billion in direct funding through the CHIPS Act. In addition to this, TXN's global manufacturing footprint should allow the firm to regionally produce chips for the region, easing the risk of supply chain disruptions or geopolitical trade tensions.

Accordingly, global data center capacity is expected to double to 200GW by 2030, creating substantial tailwinds for analog chips. Gartner is forecasting semiconductor revenue to grow to $1.3B in 2026 and to $1.5T in 2027, in part driven by the tight memory chip market environment. According to their estimates, AI semiconductors are expected to only account for 30% of the total semiconductor revenue for 2026, suggesting a large market opportunity for analog and embedded for TXN.

Silicon Laboratories Acquisition Analysis

TXN announced the acquisition of Silicon Laboratories Inc. (SLAB) on February 4, 2026, with an expected close in e1h27, in an all-cash transaction valued at $7.5b, enhancing TXN’s position in secured intelligent wireless technology. The acquisition can broaden TXN’s technology stack with a more vertically integrated offering with SLAB’s mixed signal solutions, potentially delivering stronger growth over the coming quarters. This can particularly be an appealing strategy given that more industrial devices are connected to enhance visibility into infrastructure as a driver for automation. With TXN’s internal fabrication processes, the acquisition may deliver appealing synergies of around $450 million over the coming years through insourcing chip fabrication while improving TXN’s utilization rate.

While the acquisition doesn’t appear to be margin-accretive when comparing the last twelve-month figures, I believe the driving factor will be cost reduction through integration and insourcing, as well as selling a more complete stack solution, driving greater value on a per-sale basis. For those seeking a merger-arbitrage strategy, SLAB shares can be purchased at a 7% discount to the acquisition price.

Texas Instruments Incorporated Financial Position & Forecast

Looking ahead to Q2 ’26, I’m forecasting TXN to generate $5.4b in net revenue with a diluted EPS of $1.93/share. The basis of my forecast is driven by top-line growth, delivering substantial operating leverage in the quarter and throughout the remainder of the fiscal year. Looking beyond, I believe this momentum will be sustained through at least eFY28 as power infrastructure and data center development remain as a core growth market, presenting substantial tailwinds for TXN to potentially drive growth in both volumes shipped and pricing. Given the expected growth of the addressable market, TXN’s momentum may continue through the end of the decade and beyond based on power infrastructure already being procured for delivery in the early 2030s.

TXN closed Q1 ’26 with $5.1b in cash & equivalents and marketable securities and $14b in debt on the balance sheet for a net leverage ratio of 0.99x net debt/aEBITDA, an incremental improvement from the previous quarter’s leverage ratio of 1.09x.

Risks Related to Texas Instruments Incorporated

Bull Case

TXN is in a position of strength as the industrials market picks up momentum, driven by increased power infrastructure to support large-scale data center developments. With the imminent shift towards 800 VDC in 2027, TXN could realize an uplift in pricing and content per site, particularly as data center facilities scale in capacity towards 1GW+.

Bear Case

TXN participates in a competitive, commoditized market and may not realize substantial pricing power. New data center developments are receiving substantial community pushback, potentially pushing out new projects in certain states or regions.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

TXN shares experienced a substantial run-up in the share price following the Q1 ’26 earnings release, hinting at substantial market optimism for the analog chip manufacturer. With a rapidly expanding market opportunity, I do not believe TXN shares’ growth is over but rather just getting started, particularly as the cycle is pushed out, leading me to believe that the market may be undervaluing the true growth potential of this company.

Fundamentally, I believe shares should be priced at $415/share at 23.64x eFY28 EV/aEBITDA; I am recommending TXN shares with a Strong Buy rating.

Using the model, the valuation table above references my financial forecast in the firm’s “financial position” section and ties it to the stock’s historical trading premiums. The trading premium array is derived through the normal operating cycle, with the blue-sky scenario being the stock’s peak multiple and the gray-sky scenario being the lowest point. The target multiple aims for the midpoint, or the most likely trading range for the company’s stock. The trading multiples from there are set to a probability factor based on the likelihood of the stock trading at that premium based on its historical presence. From there, the trading multiple is tied to the probability factor to derive its relative market capitalization and relative multiple.

data.title: Huntington Bancshares: The Street's Wall Of Worry Is The Big Obstacle Now
data.description:
Operating results ultimately drive long-term returns, but sentiment can be a powerful driver in the near term, and that’s a challenge for Huntington Bancshares Incorporated (HBAN) today. A series of negative guidance updates has shaken investor confidence, and there appear to be growing concerns about Huntington’s near-term organic growth prospects in increasingly competitive Southeastern banking markets. Moreover, it’s pretty common for institutional investors to steer clear of large-scale M&A stories until the integration process is largely complete.

It’s been a while since I’ve updated my thoughts on Huntington’s shares, but the stock has not done especially well in the interim. Up about 25%, HBAN shares have underperformed large bank peers, including rivals leveraged to the Southeast U.S., including Fifth Third (FITB), PNC (PNC), and Truist (TFC).

Increasing competition for both loans and deposits is a credible near-term threat, and integrating large acquisitions always carries risk. On top of that, I would not be surprised to see management take another significant swing at M&A to jumpstart its growth ambitions in Southeastern markets like North Carolina. All of that said, I think HBAN screens as one of the cheaper banks, provided that mid-single-digit core earnings growth is a credible target for the long term.

Respectable Results To Start The Year

The close of the Cadence acquisition in the first quarter renders year-over-year and sequential comparisons largely moot, but Huntington did at least manage to do a little better than expected in terms of core earnings drivers.

Revenue was about 1% better than expected, or a little less than $0.01/share relative to sell-side expectations. Net interest income (FTE basis) was about a half-penny shy of expectations, with a weaker net interest margin (3.24% vs. 3.15% in Q4’25 and sell-side expectations of 4.28%) offsetting slightly better than expected earning asset growth.

Fee-based revenue was a positive driver this quarter (and a key part of the longer-term bull thesis), driving about $0.015/share of upside. Payments and capital markets were both better than expected (by about 7% and 18%, respectively).

A higher opex guide was one of the issues coming out of the fourth quarter earnings report, but Huntington came in better than expected, with a $0.01/sh beat relative to expectations and a 58.3% adjusted efficiency ratio versus a 59.6% expectation.

Pre-provision profits were almost $0.02/share better than expected (or about 4% above sell-side estimates), and that’s a solidly positive result. The bank gave some of that back through higher provisioning (almost $0.01/share), but a lower tax rate still ultimately allowed for a nearly $0.03/share beat relative to expectations.

Guidance Once Again Creates Some Concern

Huntington’s guidance updates in 2026 have so far been one of the significant drivers of the stock’s underperformance, as they’ve mostly all been negative revisions, and the Street seems to be getting concerned as to whether there will be more bad news in the coming months.

Higher opex guidance was an issue coming out of the fourth quarter, with the company then making an intra-quarter update that included lower purchase accounting accretion expectations.

This time around, management lowered guidance to the low end of the net interest income growth range. Management mentioned some “credit philosophy misalignment” with its recently acquired banks (Cadence and Vertex) impacting loan growth but also highlighted increasing competition for loans and deposits across its markets (but likely most intense in Texas and Southeastern markets), with around 5 to 15 bp of pressure on loan spreads and higher deposit costs relative to prior expectations.

At around 85%, Huntington’s loan/deposit ratio isn’t as good as I’d like. I generally don’t get too concerned until that ratio hits 90%, but the reality is that funding limitations are emerging as a challenge for banks in Huntington’s key growth markets, and HBAN’s deposit costs were already a modest concern.

There were some positive offsets in the guidance. Fee income growth is tracking higher than expected, including the key payments and capital markets lines, and management has the flexibility to pull back on opex growth to offset the pressure on net interest income.

Growth, Offset By Elevated Risk

Considering the recent acquisitions of Vertex and Cadence and Huntington’s own organic growth drivers, there are credible reasons to expect this bank to outgrow many of its peers in the coming years. As is usually the case, though, that growth opportunity carries with it some meaningful operational and execution risks.

NIM expansion on repricing of the back book is a credible positive driver, but increased competition for loans (lower spreads) and deposits (higher funding costs) is a threat. Likewise, the bank’s focus on simultaneously building out new lending verticals and expanding its geographic reach can drive above-peer loan growth, but optimizing the balance sheet will likely work against that to some extent.

M&A synergies are another significant potential positive driver, as the company is looking for meaningful core expense synergies (25% plus) in the coming years. Although this isn’t an excessively ambitious goal, relatively large recent deals like Truist’s BB&T/SunTrust merger underscore that it’s easier to set a synergy target than reach it. Likewise, while I don’t discount potential revenue synergies from the deals (extending capital markets, payment, and wealth management services to new customers), there is fierce competition across the region in all of these markets, and retaining customers post-deal can be harder than management teams initially expect.

Another combined growth driver and risk to consider is the possibility of further meaningful M&A. Management hasn’t exactly stamped out the notion of doing more M&A, and I can certainly see how another deal or two would further accelerate the company’s organic growth plans across the Southeast U.S. (markets like North Carolina, Tennessee, Georgia, and Florida).

Given an accommodating regulatory environment today, I can see why Huntington might be inclined to move faster than they otherwise would, but the Street is often skeptical, if not outright bearish, on whole-bank M&A, and another deal close on the heels of the Cadence transaction likely won’t be a positive for sentiment.

The Outlook

I like Huntington’s overall positioning now, particularly as Cadence has skewed the bank a bit more towards commercial lending (where I see more opportunities for better risk-adjusted spreads and service-driven differentiation and growth). I also like how the bank has been prioritizing a controlled buildout of its lending capabilities (adding verticals, typically by hiring away productive loan officers from rivals) and investing in fee-based revenue generators (payments, treasury services, wealth management, and capital markets).

Execution remains the big unknown. I think Huntington has a good management team in place, but the reality is that growth through added middle-market verticals and geographical expansion into Southeastern/Texas markets is a plan that a large number of banks are following. The same is largely true of the growth initiatives in payments, treasury services, wealth management, and capital markets, as it frankly now seems unusual to find a bank in the $100-$300 billion asset tier that isn’t looking to do those things.

Why do I think Huntington can stand apart from the rivals? Strong customer satisfaction marks, like Net Promoter Score (as reported by J.D. Power) and Customer Satisfaction Scores (also reported by J.D. Power), are one important driver, as above-peer scores have in the past correlated with better growth. I also believe HBAN management has focused more narrowly than the Street may be crediting; management here has historically taken an approach of only committing to areas where they can be leaders, and I don’t believe HBAN is trying to be all things to all potential banking customers.

There are certainly some meaningful near-term risks, and HBAN management has already backed off its prior $2.00/share 2027 EPS target. Further negative revisions will not be well-received, and I do see some risk here given loan and funding competition.

That said, I’m expecting organic core earnings growth of around 6% over the next five years and around 4% thereafter. That’s sufficient to support a fair value above $19.50, as well as healthy growth in capital returns to shareholders. I get similar results from a ROTCE-driven P/TBV model (a $19.60 fair value on 17% medium-term ROTCE) and a P/E approach ($19.30 on 12x my 2026 EPS estimate).

The Bottom Line

Along with Pinnacle Financial (PNFP), Huntington stands out as one of the cheapest banks in its comp group relative to standardized approaches like P/E. While I think there are some valid reasons for conservatism (M&A integration/synergy risk, increasingly competitive markets, macro/yield curve unknowns), I also think the current discount is excessive. I don’t necessarily think that Huntington is the best bank stock to own for six-month outperformance, but for investors looking for a name to hold for a few years, I think this one merits consideration and due diligence.

data.title: Trump's Psychedelics Stance Reignites Sector - How Investors Can Benefit
data.description:
Investment Overview - White House / FDA Take Steps To Fast Track Approval Of Psychedelic Depression Meds

Last Monday, in a note for Seeking Alpha, I covered the company GH Research (GHRS), based in Dublin Ireland, and focused on the development of two drug candidates derived from mebufotenin (5-MeO-DMT), a rapid-acting psychedelic drug and serotonin agonist.

I noted the >50% rise in the company's share price across the past month, and explained that these gains were driven by the news that U.S. President Trump would be signing an executive order aimed at accelerating research and expanding access to substances used abroad to treat post-traumatic stress disorder.

A White House statement noted that over 14m American adults have a serious mental illness, with 8m currently taking prescription medicine, that suicide rates had increased by 37% between 2000 - 2018, that veterans "suffer in greater measure from this tragedy," and that:

Psychedelic drugs, including ibogaine compounds, show potential in clinical studies to address serious mental illnesses for patients whose conditions persist after completing standard therapy.

Indeed, the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) has granted Breakthrough Therapy designation to specific psychedelic drugs, and there are numerous products currently in the clinical trial pipeline for review of safety and efficacy.

It is the policy of my Administration to accelerate innovative research models and appropriate drug approvals to increase access to psychedelic drugs that could save lives and reverse the crisis of serious mental illness in America.

The Executive Order makes several pledges, perhaps most crucially, that:

The Commissioner of Food and Drugs shall provide Commissioner’s National Priority Vouchers to appropriate psychedelic drugs that have received a Breakthrough Therapy designation and are in accordance with the criteria of the National Priority Voucher Program.

This could potentially see psychedelic drugs approved in 1-2 months, as opposed to the standard review period of ~10 months after receipt of a New Drug Application ("NDA").

Furthermore, the Order states that:

The Attorney General shall, in consultation with HHS, initiate and complete review of any product containing a Schedule I substance that has successfully completed Phase 3 clinical trials for a serious mental health disorder, so that rescheduling, if appropriate under 21 U.S.C. 811, may proceed as quickly as practicable for such specific products that are ultimately approved under section 505 of the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act.

Again, this is crucial because psychedelic drugs are classified as schedule I substances, which means that they are considered to have no medical use, and may not be prescribed, marketed or sold in the United States.

Without rescheduling, a psychedelic drug product would stand almost no chance of securing commercial approval, but now it seems as though the government is opening the door for multiple companies developing such products to push to have their drugs approved.

Besides GH Research, there are several listed companies investors should be aware of whose chance of becoming commercially successful have drastically improved following last week's news. For example, Compass Pathways (CMPS), AtaiBeckley (ATAI), Definium Therapeutics (DFTX), and Cybin (HELP).

In a statement last week, the FDA commented that:

The FDA is issuing national priority vouchers to three companies studying:

  • Psilocybin for treatment-resistant depression.
  • Psilocybin for major depressive disorder.
  • Methylone for post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD).

In addition, the agency is allowing an early phase clinical study of noribogaine hydrochloride to move forward following an Investigational New Drug (IND) submission.

In the rest of this post, I will identify and discuss the companies in question, and speculate about which may present the optimal investment opportunity for an investor seeking to gain access to a sector of the stock market that appears to have been handed a new lease of life.

FDA Hands Priority Review Vouchers To Three Biotechs, Including Compass Therapeutics

Last Friday, it was reported that the FDA would hand out Commissioner's National Priority Vouchers ("CNPVs") to three biotechs - Compass Pathways (CMPS), the non-profit Usona Institute, and Transcend Therapeutics - a private biotech that was acquired by Otsuka Pharmaceutical in March.

The obvious choice here would therefore be Compass - its ADR stock is now up >35% year-to-date, and >140% on a one-year basis (at the time of writing), trading at $9.6 pre-market today. Compass' market cap valuation, however, is $920m, and on a five-year basis, it's stock is in fact down >70%.

On Friday, Compass issued a press release, confirming receipt of a CNPV - it's CEO was quoted as follows:

As the most advanced company in the classic psychedelics field, Compass has generated positive data from two large, well controlled Phase 3 clinical trials. Based on the strength of our data, the FDA granted us a rolling NDA submission and review.

Importantly, while the CNPV may provide process efficiencies and accelerated review timelines, an NDA submission must still meet FDA’s established standards of clinical evidence, scientific rigor, and regulatory compliance. We are confident we meet these standards.

Compass's product candidate COMP360 is a synthetic form of psilocybin, a naturally occurring psychedelic compound produced by over 200 species of fungi. When I covered the company for Seeking Alpha in February, it had just announced positive Phase 3 study results for COMP260 in patients with treatment resistant depression ("TRD").

The primary endpoint was the difference in change from baseline in the Montgomery-Åsberg Depression Rating Scale (MADRS) scores between the 25 mg and 1 mg groups at Week 6, and the company noted that:

Two fixed doses – administered 3 weeks apart – of COMP360 25 mg versus 1 mg demonstrated a highly statistically significant reduction in symptom severity with a p-value of <0.001 and a clinically meaningful difference of -3.8 points in change at the primary endpoint.

While this may be sufficient to secure an approval, doubts around the efficacy of COMP360 have led to market sell-offs in the past, and analysts have noted (as discussed in my last post) that Johnson & Johnson's (JNJ) Spravato, a form of esketamine, approved to treat TRD in 2019 (and as a monotherapy last year), may have a slight edge in this regard.

Besides non-drug related therapeutic options such as cognitive behavioural therapy ("CBT"), and stimulation therapies such as vagus nerve stimulation, and electroconvulsive therapy, Spravato is one of the few options available to treat TRD, and earned revenues of ~$1.7bn in 2025.

The drug can be administered by a licensed healthcare practitioner, with patients monitored for 2-hours post-administration, while patients undergoing therapy with COMP-360 must be monitored for 6 hours, and meet with a therapist prior to treatment.

As we can see above, Compass believes its product may serve a 4m patient market, however there are some restrictions on its growth worth pointing out, for example, a lack of education about the benefits of treatment with psychedelics amongst patients and physicians, a protracted treatment and monitoring process, and a paucity of treatment centers available at this time.

Nevertheless, Compass management must now feel that a potential approval is tantalizingly close, and if its therapy is reclassified from Schedule I to Schedule II, III, or IV, it would be far more simple to transport, distribute, and administer its therapy, and with government backing, to market it to physicians and patients also.

I'd also speculate that insurers would be open to providing reimbursement for COMP360, if approved, and although I am not aware of any discussion around the price of the therapy, Spravato apparently costs ~$4k - $6k uninsured in the first month (when ~8 doses are administered), the $1k - $1.8k, or ~$500 - $1k in the first month, and $200 - $600 thereafter.

Given COMP-360 lasts "at least 26 weeks after 1 or 2 doses (source: compass corporate presentation), I wonder if the therapy could potentially undercut Spravato in terms of annual cost?

It may be a close run thing, but either way, I don't find it hard to plot a course to a "blockbuster" (>$1bn per annum) market opportunity for psychedelic drug treatment for TRD patients, and potentially, for patients with other forms of depression, such as major depressive disorder, an indication in which AbbVie's (ABBV) med Vraylar earned ~$3.6bn of revenues last year.

As such, my take is that, while approval is by no means guaranteed - Compass itself points out the FDA's review process is likely to be no less rigorous despite being shorter - Compass <$1bn market cap valuation at the time of writing may significantly undervalue the business, and I'd expect plenty of share price volatility, trending to the upside, as the company shares more updates on the progress of any approval application.

Other Competitors That May Challenge Compass, GH Research

While Compass' receipt of a CNPV may put it in pole position to secure a historic first approval for a psychedelic med, there are many other companies that will be hoping to secure their own CNPVs before long.

For example, AtaiBeckley stock is now up >35% on a one-month basis, and up >200% on a one-year basis. I covered the company in October last year, assigning stock a Buy rating, although shares are in fact down ~10% since.

ATAI has two candidates targeting patients with TRD - BPL-003, a mebufotenin nasal spray, and VLS-01, a DMT buccal film. The company plans to initiate a Phase 3 study for BPL-003 this quarter after completing an end-of-phase 2 meeting with the FDA. In its Phase 2a, open label proof of concept study, the company observed that:

Following the first (8 mg) dose, patients experienced a mean MADRS reduction of 13.3 points from baseline at Day 2 and a mean MADRS reduction of 12.9 at Day 8. One week after the second dose (12 mg), there was a further decrease in MADRS score to a total of a 19.0-point reduction from baseline, with sustained antidepressant effects observed through Week 12 (13.7 points from baseline).

Apparently, patients in the study were able to be discharged 2 hours after therapy, while the efficacy data reported makes a case for a best-in-class therapy, although cross-trial comparisons are generally problematic to make, and not always to be trusted.

In its latest earnings statement, Atai says its twin Phase 3 studies will read out topline data in 2029, which potentially places any approval years away, and years after a potential COMP-360 approval. The company reported a cash position of $85m and short-term securities of $135m as of the end of 2025, which it says is sufficient to see it to the Phase 3 readouts.

While time may be against Atai, its data shared to date does seem to imply it has a shot of one-day challenging for standard-of-care ("SoC") within an emerging psychedelics field. Its market cap is presently ~$1.7bn, which may reflect a more diverse pipeline than e.g. Compass, targeting social anxiety disorder ("SAD") with an MDMA based drug, and sharing Phase 2 TRD data for VLS-01 later this year.

Meanwhile, Definium Therapeutics, formerly known as MindMed, has seen its stock increase by >205% since I issued stock with a Hold rating back in January 2025. The company's lead product is DT120 ODT - lysergide tartrate, the salt form of lysergic acid diethylamide ("LSD"), a synthetic serotonergic psychedelic.

In an investor day press release from earlier this month, Definium's CEO stated:

With three pivotal readouts expected over the next six months, we are rapidly establishing comprehensive clinical evidence for DT120 ODT. Together, these trial outcomes will inform our regulatory approach, including an expeditious path to a potential NDA submission. We continue to execute with focus and urgency to deliver transformative treatments for patients and sustained value for shareholders.”

The readouts are in MDD, Generalized Anxiety Disorder ("GAD"), with another readout in post traumatic stress disorder ("PTSD") due 2027. The company reported a cash position of $412m in a recent investor presentation, and full-year 2025 operating expenses of $166.3m, therefore, although additional fundraisings may be conducted if upcoming data readouts are positive, the company has no immediate financial concerns.

As we can see above, Definium's data in GAD appears to compare favorably to drugs such as Eli Lilly's (LLY) Cymbalta, a drug whose sales are now admittedly negligible due to its patents having expired many years ago. Nevertheless, with its novel mechanism of action ("MoA"), I'd expect DT120 to command a much higher price point than older therapies, with patent protections potentially stretching out many years, and in markets such as GAD and MDD, again, a blockbuster revenue opportunity seems to present itself.

I'd speculate that Definium could be pushing for a CNPV in 2026, although with its market cap now standing at $2.24bn, it's unclear how much more upside shares could explore.

Finally, Cybin (HELP), which has recently rebranded to Helus Pharma, issued a statement in response to President Trump's Executive Order last week. The CEO is quoted as follows:

Our lead program, HLP003, currently in Phase 3 development, has received FDA Breakthrough Therapy designation for the adjunctive treatment of major depressive disorder, reflecting the urgent need for improved treatments for depression, including the ongoing epidemic of suicide in the United States

Its lead product is a deuterated Novel Serotonergic Agonists ("NSA"), HLP003 which will report data from a Phase 3 study in adjunctive treatment of MDD in Q4, according to a corporate presentation. This therapy is orally administered, although a second candidate, administered intramuscular, targets GAD.

The company's CEO recently stepped down, at the request of the Board of Directors, with executive chairman Eric So resuming his role as interim chief executive officer.

As we can see above, management believes its deuterated candidate offers plenty of competitive advantages over psilocybin, and says HLP003 has achieved a mean 23-point reduction in MADRS at 12 months in a Phase 2 study.

The company reported a cash position of $195m as of the end of 2025, a a net loss of $(42.7m) for Q4 2025. The Phase 3 results due Q4 2026 would appear critical to the company's chances of both raising fresh funding, and securing a CNPV, perhaps early in 2027.

Concluding Thoughts - A Psychedelic To Suit Every Taste?

In summary, then, last week's news has reignited a sector of the stock market that was weighed down by the classification of psychedelics as Schedule I substances, which seemed to make any approval submission from any company unlikely to succeed, and the commercial opportunity significantly restricted.

Now, it seems possible that several therapies could see their approval submissions fast-tracked, and with so many Americans seeking treatment for mental conditions that physicians and pharmaceutical companies struggle to find effective therapies for, the market opportunity in front of the companies covered in this post seems undeniable.

Which company should an investor pick, however? It is difficult to say, and in this post I have tried to be objective and explain each companies position in terms of the progress of their therapies, data shared to date, and upcoming catalysts.

I would finally summarize the opportunities in play as follows. Compass targets TRD, a smaller market than MDD, or even GAD, perhaps, but still large enough to support blockbuster revenues in my view, and it has secured its CNPV. The company has ~$150m funding, which management says will last into 2028, and its stock and valuation are substantially lower than the other companies discussed. Therefore, investors willing to take on some risk and with a short-term investment horizon may find this opportunity suits them best.

ATAI has multiple candidates, strong efficacy data (in my view), and a 2-hour treatment window, but a longer wait for pivotal study data, with sufficient funds to last into 2029, when it could launch an approval submission. It's higher market cap and lengthy studies seem to imply that sudden upside is less likely, but as a longer-term buy and hold, it certainly intrigues.

Likewise, Definium's higher market cap may make sudden share price spikes less likely, although its target markets are larger and more diverse, data strong, and near-term catalysts intriguing - this feels like arguably the least risky longer-term play.

Managerial overhauls and limited funding may count against Cybin / Helus, however a major data catalyst due Q4 could open up a quick path to approval.

Finally, as I wrote about GH Research last week:

GH Research is now confident that it has a "best-in-class" TRD therapeutic treatment, a best-in-class psychedelic, and a best-in-class molecule, and the company believes that, should GH001 be approved, it would qualify for five years' exclusivity in the U.S. (i.e., no generic versions for GH001 could be launched by competitors) and ten years in Europe if approved in that territory, while the product would also be patent protected, including its novel use as an inhalable product, its aerosol composition, and as a medical device.

In summary, there is plenty for prospective investors to mull over, and I would urge them to conduct their own due diligence, as this note provides overviews only, and each candidate provides a different package of efficacy / safety, convenience, durability, and stage of development.

It's also important to note that a new government administration may have the power to row back on the Executive Order, and be more circumspect around changing Schedule's from I to II, III, or IV, and that this could lead to sharp sell-offs in these companies stock prices.

Nevertheless, Psychedelics has become a hot space to watch, where once it seemed obstacles to approval may be too great. I'm excited to see how these various companies plans play out, and at this stage, I'd be watching Compass most closely, while paying close attention to the Phase 3 readouts for Definium and Cybin / Helus.
